Why This Institute Exists

1,400 years of drift.

The Qur'an tells us something happened to Moses's people in just 40 days. While he was away receiving guidance on the mountain, they reverted to the same idol worship they had just been saved from. God put that story in the Book — not as ancient history — but as a direct warning for everyone who comes after.

Now consider what happens over 1,400 years. Political dynasties needing obedient populations. Clergy paid to maintain the status quo. Hadith compiled by men born 200 years after the Prophet died. Zoroastrian and pagan rituals rebranded as Islam. Scholars elevated to the level of lords. The Qur'an set aside.

The Qur'an calls itself complete, detailed, and sufficient. It was the first thing they abandoned.

— The Central Question

We started this institute because we read the Qur'an and couldn't find most of what we were told was mandatory. So we went back to the source — the Arabic text, the root words, the historical record. What we found changed everything.

Prophet Moses left for 40 days. His people built an idol. The ones who believed in him returned to their old ways in less than six weeks.

That's 40 days. We've had 1,400 years.

The drift from the Qur'an wasn't sudden. It was gradual, systematic, and deliberately engineered — as we document throughout this site.

"And He lays corruption upon those who do not reason."
Qur'an 10:100
What We Actually Found

Salat Is Not
Ritual Prayer

This is one of the most important findings in our research. The word "Salat" has been completely mistranslated and misunderstood — and here is exactly how we know.

What Salat Actually Means

We went through all 102 occurrences of the word "Salat" in the Qur'an. Not one of them describes specific physical postures. Not one mentions specific daily timings. Not one refers to the specific Arabic phrases recited in traditional prayer. This is verifiable — you can check it yourself.

The Arabic root of Salat is ص-ل-و (S-L-W). According to Lane's Lexicon — the definitive classical Arabic dictionary — this root means: to follow closely, to pursue, to remain attached, to communicate, to connect. In classical Arabic, when a horse in a race follows so closely behind the lead horse that its head overlaps the first horse's flank, that second horse is called "Al-Mussalli" — the one performing salat. It means close connection and following.

The Qur'an also pairs "Salat" with "Sabr" (patience) as abstract concepts working together (2:45, 2:153). In Arabic grammar, when a single verb connects two nouns, both nouns must be either abstract or concrete. Sabr is clearly an abstract concept — not a physical ritual. Therefore Salat cannot be a physical ritual either. This is a grammatical proof, not an opinion.

What was Salat during the Prophet's time? Based on Qur'anic evidence, Salat sessions were guided gatherings — educational, communicative meetings where divine messages were delivered, discussed, and applied. The audience was urged to understand and reason through the messages, not to perform physical movements. These were essentially what we would today call study circles and community assemblies focused on establishing divine guidance in practice.

What replaced it? Aurangzaib Yousufzai's research documents that Namaaz — the five-times daily ritual prayer — has roots in pre-Islamic Zoroastrian practice and was institutionalized by the Umayyad dynasty as a mechanism of social control. Today, even a hadith in Bukhari records a companion of the Prophet saying: "I do not find things as they were at the time of the Prophet." When asked about prayer, he said: "Have you not done in the prayer what you have done?" (Bukhari 9/8) — an early witness to the distortion happening in real time.

Evidence 1: Salat prevents wrongdoing

The Qur'an says salat "prevents indecency and wrongdoing" (29:45). If salat is a five-minute physical exercise, how does it prevent wrongdoing? But if salat is a comprehensive system of living according to divine guidance — the prevention makes complete sense.

Qur'an 29:45

Evidence 2: Birds perform salat

Qur'an 24:41 says birds perform salat. Birds don't stand, bow, and prostrate in prayer. But birds absolutely do exhibit close connection with, and following of, divine patterns in nature — which is what salat actually means.

Qur'an 24:41

Evidence 3: Wartime salat makes no sense as prayer

Qur'an 4:101-103 describes salat during wartime — with weapons, in shifts, with some standing guard. If salat were a ritual with required postures and conditions, this passage would be incomprehensible. As a cautionary advisory session, it makes perfect sense.

Qur'an 4:101-103

Evidence 4: You restore missed salat through reform

If you miss a ritual prayer, you make it up by praying again. But Qur'an 19:59-60 says those who let salat lapse restore it through "doing good works" — not through more prayer. This only makes sense if salat means a system of righteous conduct, not a ritual prayer schedule.

Qur'an 19:59-60
How It Actually Happened

The Step-by-Step Replacement
of the Qur'an's Message

This wasn't a sudden change. It was a deliberate, staged process that took roughly 300 years. Based on the research of Dr. Sayed Abdul Wadud and Aurangzaib Yousufzai, here is what we found.

1
Year 40 AH — The First Break
The Pious Caliphate ends. The Umayyad dynasty begins.
In 40 AH, the Umayyad clan — the old pre-Islamic Meccan nobility — seized control of the Islamic state. This was the moment the rot began. The Umayyads had resisted Islam until the conquest of Mecca. Now they were running it. Their interests: maintain political power, prevent the Qur'an's radical message of equality and justice from threatening the elite. Their method: redefine Islam so it couldn't threaten anyone.
Qur'an 6:159 — "Those who divided their religion into sects — you have nothing to do with them."
2
50–100 AH — The Ritual Injection
Namaaz is introduced as mandatory "Islamic" prayer.
The five-times daily Namaaz ritual — adapted from Zoroastrian practice — was gradually imposed under official sponsorship. Mosques, previously centers of governance and community learning, were emptied of their political function and turned into ritual spaces. The ruling class moved into palaces. Clergy were installed in mosques. Their job: keep people praying, not thinking. The Qur'an's command to establish Salat (a comprehensive system of righteous conduct) was reduced to a physical ritual that required nothing from you except your body.
Qur'an 107:4–7 — Condemns those who perform prayer while neglecting the needy and doing harm.
3
100–200 AH — The Fabrication Industry
Hadith is invented at scale under Umayyad and Abbasid sponsorship.
Persian scholars with Zoroastrian and Jewish backgrounds — hired by the court — produced hadith by the hundreds of thousands. Bukhari himself gathered 600,000 reports and rejected all but 7,000 as too unreliable to publish. The first four Caliphs had all forbidden hadith collection. Now it became an industry. These hadith did three things: justified the rituals being introduced, elevated the Prophet to a near-divine status requiring worship, and gave clerics the authority to replace the Qur'an as the source of Islamic law.
Qur'an 77:50 — "In what hadith after this will they believe?"
4
200–300 AH — The Canonization
Fiqh schools solidify. The Qur'an becomes ceremonial.
The four major Fiqh schools (Hanafi, Maliki, Shafi'i, Hanbali) were established and canonized. Their rulings — originally meant as flexible by-laws — were declared permanently binding on all Muslims. The "gates of ijtihad" (independent reasoning from the Qur'an) were declared closed. No more original thinking allowed. The Qur'an became a book people memorized, kissed, kept on high shelves, and never actually applied as law. Dr. Wadud calls this "Quranocracy replaced by human sovereignty."
Qur'an 9:31 — "They have taken their scholars and priests as lords besides God."
5
300+ AH — The Complete Replacement
Sufism, saint worship, and sectarianism complete the picture.
Sufism — rooted in Greek Neo-Platonism and Hindu mysticism — entered through the back door, replacing the Qur'an's activist call with passive inner experience and devotion to shrines. Saints became intercessors. Shrines replaced direct connection with God. Sects multiplied. Today the result is visible: hundreds of millions performing five-times daily Namaaz faithfully while their societies collapse under injustice, corruption, and misery — the exact torments the Qur'an warned about for those who abandon its guidance.
Qur'an 25:30 — "And the Messenger will say: My Lord, my people have abandoned this Qur'an."
"When it is said to them, follow what God has revealed — they say: We will follow what we found our fathers doing."
Qur'an 2:170
Dr. Sayed Abdul Wadud's Research

Six Documented Ways
the Message Was Corrupted

These aren't theories. They are documented historical processes — each verifiable and each confirmed by the Qur'anic text itself.

01
First Corruption

God replaced with human intermediaries

The direct relationship between a person and God was broken by inserting human middlemen. Messengers became objects of worship. Priests became required go-betweens. Pirs, saints, Imams, and scholars became the authorities people submitted to. The Qur'an is clear: sovereignty belongs to God alone. No human stands between you and your Creator — not even the Prophet.

"They have taken their scholars and priests as lords besides God." — 9:31
02
Second Corruption

Islam was split into sects from within

A two-pronged operation: political weakening through manufactured sectarian division, and ideological corruption through fabricated hadith, false interpretation, and cultural infiltration from Persian, Jewish, and Zoroastrian traditions. The result: hundreds of competing sects each claiming exclusive truth, none returning to the Book that started it all.

"Those who divided their religion into sects — you are not of them in anything." — 6:159
03
Third Corruption

The Qur'anic text was attacked

Certain sectarian groups promoted the idea that the present Qur'an is corrupted, that its words carry hidden meanings only Imams can access, and that the real text was suppressed. The Qur'an demolishes this. God personally guaranteed its preservation (15:9). The Prophet confirmed its completeness before he died.

"Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and We will surely preserve it." — 15:9
04
Fourth Corruption

Sufism replaced action with passivity

Sufism entered wearing Islamic terminology, but its roots are in Greek philosophy, Neo-Platonism, and Hindu mysticism. It replaced the Qur'an's call to reform the world — establish justice, stand against oppression — with passive inner experience and devotion to saints. One of the most effective tools ever used to neutralize Islamic social reform.

"Do you order people to righteousness while you forget yourselves?" — 2:44
05
Fifth Corruption

Namaaz replaced Salat

The Umayyad rulers needed a compliant population. The solution: take the Qur'an's command to establish Salat (a comprehensive system of righteous conduct and community guidance), reduce it to a five-minute physical ritual borrowed from Zoroastrian practice, and impose it five times a day. People feel religious. Nothing changes. Justice goes unestablished. The system is preserved.

"Establish salat — salat prevents indecency and wrongdoing." — 29:45
06
Sixth Corruption — Quranocracy

Human sovereignty replaced God's

The Umayyad dynasty imported the Christian "divine right of kings" into Islam, calling rulers "shadows of God on earth." Wadud's Quranocracy demonstrates that democracy, autocracy, theocracy, and clergy-run systems all share the same fatal flaw: they place sovereignty in human hands. The Qur'an says the command belongs to God alone, exercised through His Book — not dynasties or man-made constitutions.

"The command is for none but God." — 12:40
"Most of them follow nothing but assumption. And assumption can never replace the truth."
Qur'an 10:36
A Critical Key to Understanding the Qur'an

Parables and Allegories:
What the Qur'an Itself Says

"My point is not that those ancient people told literal stories and we are now smart enough to take them symbolically — but that they told them symbolically and we are now dumb enough to take them literally."
— John Dominic Crossan, Biblical Scholar

The Qur'an explicitly describes itself as containing parables — stories told symbolically to teach deeper truths. The Arabic word is Mathal (مثل). The Qur'an uses this word to describe its own stories as moral analogies, not literal history. When it says "these are parables for people who think" (29:43), it is giving you the reading instruction directly.

What the Qur'an Says About Its Own Stories

The Qur'an introduces Noah's story (11:24) as a parable and then immediately follows it with a reminder of its allegorical nature. The flood narrative in chapter 29 is specifically described as one of "the parables, the meaning of which can be grasped by none except the knowledgeable" (29:43).

Noah in the Qur'an is not primarily a historical person. He is a human archetype — a symbol of every truth-seeker navigating a society that rejects divine guidance. His Ark is not a literal boat. It is a metaphor for the divine guidance itself, carrying those who attach themselves to it through the floods of worldly desires and intellectual shallowness.

When you understand this, the Qur'an opens up completely differently. The stories of Abraham, Moses, Jesus, the bird symbolism, the descriptions of heaven and hell — these are not CNN news reports from ancient times. They are sophisticated symbolic frameworks designed to teach eternal principles about human consciousness, social reform, and divine guidance.

"And these are the parables We set forth for mankind, but none will understand them except those who have knowledge."

— Qur'an 29:43

"We have set forth for people in this Qur'an every kind of parable so that they might take heed."

— Qur'an 39:27

"There is certainly in their stories a lesson for people of understanding. This is not a fabricated hadith — it is a confirmation of what came before it, a detailed explanation of all things, and guidance and mercy for a people who believe."

— Qur'an 12:111

Qur'an 25:30
Hadith Refutations

15 Arguments
for Hadith.
15 Qur'anic Answers.

Every standard argument made for hadith authority — answered directly from the Qur'an. Not our interpretation. The actual text.

"Most of them follow nothing but conjecture. And conjecture can never replace the truth."

— Qur'an 10:36

Bukhari was born 194 years after the Prophet. Muslim was born 206 years after. The Qur'an asks: "Do not follow what you have no knowledge of" (17:36).

01
The Argument
"Obeying the Messenger means following Hadith"
The Qur'an defines what obeying the Messenger means: obeying the revelation he delivered. The Prophet was himself commanded to follow only what was revealed (6:50). His duty was to convey — not to create a second religious source. No verse equates obeying the Messenger with obeying books compiled two centuries after his death.
Qur'an 24:54 · 6:50 · 5:99
02
The Argument
"The Messenger explained the Qur'an — so Hadith contains his explanations"
The word in 16:44 is "li-tubayyina" — to make evident, not to add external content. The Qur'an says its verses are perfected (11:1) and it clarifies itself (25:33). The Prophet delivered the Qur'an — he didn't produce a parallel religious source alongside it.
Qur'an 16:44 · 25:33 · 11:1
03
The Argument
"Hadith provides the details of Salat and Zakat the Qur'an doesn't give"
"We did not leave anything out of the Book" (6:38). When you understand Salat through its actual Arabic root — not through 300-year-old ritual assumptions — the Qur'an is entirely sufficient. "This Book is a clarification for all things" (16:89).
Qur'an 6:38 · 29:45 · 16:89
04
The Argument
"The Qur'an is too general — Hadith fills in the necessary specifics"
The Qur'an calls itself "mufassal" — fully detailed (41:3). It calls itself "tibyan" — clarification for all things (16:89). God asks: "Is it not sufficient for them that We sent down the Book?" (29:51). If God says it is sufficient, claiming it needs supplementing contradicts His own word.
Qur'an 29:51 · 41:3 · 6:114
05
The Argument
"God preserved Hadith as part of preserving the religion"
God promises to preserve exactly one thing: "Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and We will surely preserve it" (15:9). The Reminder is the Qur'an. No divine preservation was promised for any other source. Hadith is a human compilation. God never guaranteed its accuracy.
Qur'an 15:9
06
The Argument
"The Prophet had independent legal authority beyond the Qur'an"
The Prophet himself said: "I do not follow except what is revealed to me" (6:50). He judged only by God's revelation (5:48). The Qur'an warns him against following personal desires or tradition when making judgments (5:49). His authority was the Qur'an — nothing else.
Qur'an 6:50 · 5:48 · 5:49
07
The Argument
"The companions followed Hadith — so should we"
The Qur'an explicitly rejects this reasoning: "They followed their forefathers even if they understood nothing" (2:170). "If you obey most of those on Earth, they will mislead you from God's path" (6:116). Truth is not determined by historical precedent. It is determined by what God revealed.
Qur'an 2:170 · 6:116
08
The Argument
"Rejecting Hadith means rejecting the Prophet himself"
"The Messenger's duty is only to convey clearly" (5:99). Accepting the Prophet's mission means accepting the Qur'an he conveyed. Verse 59:7 — misused constantly for this argument — addresses the distribution of war spoils in a specific historical situation. It is not a general mandate for hadith authority.
Qur'an 5:99 · 59:7
09
The Argument
"Isnad science verifies which Hadith are authentic"
Chains of oral narrators spanning 200 years remain human conjecture — not divine preservation. "Do not follow what you have no knowledge of" (17:36). "They only follow conjecture, and conjecture is no substitute for truth" (53:28). No chain of human memory equals a divine guarantee.
Qur'an 17:36 · 53:28
10
The Argument
"Hadith fills the gaps the Qur'an left open"
"We did not leave anything out of the Book" (6:38). "Today I have completed your system for you" (5:3). God declares the system complete and nothing omitted. Claiming there are gaps contradicts His own declaration. You cannot simultaneously believe 5:3 and claim the Qur'an is insufficient.
Qur'an 6:38 · 5:3
11
The Argument
"Sunnah is a second form of divine revelation"
53:3-4 refers to the Qur'an itself being recited — not personal sayings. The Prophet stated he followed only revelation (6:50). If Sunnah were revelation, God would have preserved it the same way He preserved the Qur'an. He did not. That absence is the evidence.
Qur'an 53:3–4 · 6:50 · 15:9
12
The Argument
"The Prophet's legal judgments prove law exists outside the Qur'an"
"So judge between them by what God has sent down" (5:48). "Do not follow their desires over the truth that has come to you" (5:49). The Qur'an commands the Prophet to judge only by revelation and warns him against tradition or personal preference. Every judgment was Qur'anic.
Qur'an 5:48 · 5:49
13
The Argument
"Scholarly consensus (Ijma) confirms Hadith authority"
"If you follow most of those on Earth, they will mislead you from God's path" (6:116). "They have taken their scholars and priests as lords besides God" (9:31). The Qur'an doesn't just fail to support this — it directly and explicitly warns against exactly this kind of reasoning.
Qur'an 6:116 · 9:31
14
The Argument
"The same community preserved both the Qur'an and the Hadith"
"Indeed, We have sent down the Reminder, and We will surely preserve it" (15:9). God preserves the Qur'an — not human transmitters. The Qur'an never equates human sincerity with divine preservation. These are categorically different claims.
Qur'an 15:9
15
The Argument
"You simply cannot practice Islam without Hadith"
"This Qur'an guides to that which is most upright" (17:9). "We have sent the Book as clarification for all things" (16:89). "Hold fast to what We have revealed to you" (43:43). The Qur'an declares itself practical, complete, and sufficient. Claiming otherwise contradicts God's own words about His own Book.
Qur'an 17:9 · 16:89 · 43:43
"Do they not reflect upon the Qur'an? Had it been from other than God, they would have found in it many contradictions."
Qur'an 4:82

About This Institute

Not tradition.
Not ritual.
Understanding.

We're based out of Columbus, Ohio. This institute started because we read the Qur'an and couldn't find most of what we were told was mandatory. So we studied the Arabic. Traced every root word. Read Wadud, Yousufzai, Siraj Islam, and Joseph Islam's research. Went through the historical record on hadith compilation, Umayyad political history, and the origins of traditional Islamic practice.

What we found wasn't surprising once we saw it clearly: most of what is practiced as Islam today was constructed 200 to 300 years after the Prophet died, by people with political and financial interests in replacing the Qur'an's authority with their own.

Every claim we make is backed by a specific verse. Every argument can be tested. We don't ask you to take our word for it — we ask you to read the Qur'an.
